metadata.title: Why a 50-turn agent pays for the same screenshot 35 times unless it caches the pixels
metadata.purpose: Hash the screenshot, mark it `cache_control={"type":"ephemeral"}` on first send; later identical turns return a cache hit — instant, zero new tokens — instead of reprocessing.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: tokens, LLM API calls, caching semantics
metadata.exclusions: full caching protocol, cache eviction policies
metadata.visual_object: A 50-cell filmstrip where repeated frames light green (cache hit) and only the 5 unique frames light red (tokenized)
metadata.hook: Every agent turn snaps a fresh screenshot, but the desktop rarely changed — re-tokenizing identical pixels burns tokens and latency for no new information.

beats.narration_text: A 50-turn form-filling task revisits only 5 unique desktop states. Naive: 50 × 2,000 = 100,000 image tokens. With ephemeral cache_control on first sight of each state: 5 × 2,000 = 10,000 tokens; the other 45 turns are cache hits.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: If most screenshots in a long task are unchanged, why re-tokenize them instead of reusing the first?
Every agent turn snaps a fresh screenshot, but the desktop rarely changed — re-tokenizing identical pixels burns tokens and latency for no new information.
Hash the screenshot, mark it `cache_control={"type":"ephemeral"}` on first send; later identical turns return a cache hit — instant, zero new tokens — instead of reprocessing.
beats.visual: A 50-cell filmstrip where repeated frames light green (cache hit) and only the 5 unique frames light red (tokenized)
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: States A A A B B C C C A A… First visit to each = 2,000 tokens; every revisit = 0. Total 10,000 vs 100,000.
Hash the screenshot, mark it `cache_control={"type":"ephemeral"}` on first send; later identical turns return a cache hit — instant, zero new tokens — instead of reprocessing.
